+++ b/docs/STABILITY.md
@@ -0,0 +1,118 @@
+---
+title: SKF Stability & Public API Contract
+description: What SemVer covers at v1.0.0 — the surfaces downstream consumers may pin against, and the surfaces explicitly excluded.
+---
+
+> **Status:** DRAFT — locks at v1.0.0 cut (Story 5.1 pre-flight audit). Review-window open until then.
+
+This document is the public API contract for `bmad-module-skill-forge` at v1.0.0. It enumerates exactly which SKF surfaces [Semantic Versioning 2.0.0](https://semver.org/) covers and which are explicitly `@internal`. The goal is that a downstream consumer pinning `"bmad-module-skill-forge": "^1.0.0"` can identify — unambiguously — whether a given SKF change is breaking, additive, or internal. Breaking changes to covered surfaces require a major-version bump; additive changes to covered surfaces are minor bumps; changes to `@internal` surfaces are free to land in any release.
+
+## Covered Surfaces
+
+The five buckets below are the entirety of the v1.0.0 public surface. Anything not explicitly listed here is `@internal` (see [§ @internal — Not Covered by SemVer](#internal--not-covered-by-semver)).
+
+### CLI Command Surface
+
+The CLI is invoked via `npx bmad-module-skill-forge <subcommand>` (one-shot) or `npm run skf:<subcommand>` (post-install). Covered:
+
+- **Subcommands**: `install`, `update`, `status`, `uninstall`. Renaming, removing, or changing the semantics of any subcommand is breaking.
+- **Top-level options**: `-V, --version` (prints the installed package version) and `-h, --help` (prints command help). Provided by commander as part of the entrypoint at `tools/cli/skf-cli.js`.
+- **Per-subcommand flags**: none are currently part of the v1.0.0 surface. Each subcommand today accepts no flags. Adding a new flag is additive (minor); removing or renaming a flag once added would be breaking.
+- **Exit codes**: `0` on success (including user-cancel on `install` and "nothing to do" paths on `uninstall`); `1` on unhandled failure. Intermediate exit codes are not part of the contract.
+- **`npm run skf:*` script aliases**: `skf:install`, `skf:update`, `skf:status`, `skf:uninstall` in `package.json` are the post-install convenience entry points. Renaming or removing any `skf:*` script is breaking. (Note: `release:*` scripts are NOT part of this contract — they are internal-to-maintainers and are being retired across Epic 4 and Epic 6.)
+
+### Programmatic API of `tools/cli/skf-cli.js`
+
+`tools/cli/skf-cli.js` is the CLI entrypoint for `npx bmad-module-skill-forge` and for `npm run skf:<subcommand>`. Though `package.json`'s `main` field points at this file (standard npm resolution for bin-like packages), it has **no programmatic export surface**. The file runs commander setup and calls `program.parse(process.argv)` as top-level side effects; it has no `module.exports` block. A `require('bmad-module-skill-forge')` from Node runs the CLI parser against the caller's `process.argv` — not a supported usage.
+
+Programmatic embedding of SKF is explicitly `@internal` at v1.0.0. If a programmatic API is ever added, it will be introduced via an explicit `module.exports` block and a minor version bump (additive).
+
+### Skill Manifest & Frontmatter Contract
+
+**`SKILL.md` frontmatter.** SKF-shipped `SKILL.md` files conform to the [agentskills.io minimal frontmatter spec](https://github.com/agentskills/agentskills). Required fields are `name` (kebab-case) and `description` (one-line natural-language summary that terminates with an explicit trigger phrase — see the `src/skf-*/SKILL.md` files in this repo for live examples). SKF does NOT extend the agentskills.io spec; there are no SKF-specific frontmatter fields. If the agentskills.io spec evolves, SKF tracks upstream rather than forking.
+
+**`bmad-skill-manifest.yaml` schema.** The agent-type skill manifest shape SKF uses is validated by the zod schema at `test/schema/agent.js`:
+
+```js
+const manifestSchema = z
+  .object({
+    type: z.enum(['agent', 'workflow', 'tool']),   // required
+    name: createNonEmptyString('name'),            // required
+    displayName: createNonEmptyString('displayName').optional(),
+    title: createNonEmptyString('title').optional(),
+    icon: z.string().optional(),
+    capabilities: z.string().optional(),
+    role: createNonEmptyString('role').optional(),
+    identity: createNonEmptyString('identity').optional(),
+    communicationStyle: createNonEmptyString('communicationStyle').optional(),
+    principles: createNonEmptyString('principles').optional(),
+    module: createNonEmptyString('module'),        // required
+  })
+  .strict();
+```
+
+The schema is strict — unknown keys are rejected. Renaming or removing a required field, changing an enum value, or tightening an optional field into required is breaking. Adding a new optional field is additive. The `module` field is always `skf` for SKF-authored manifests.
+
+### Installation Layout
+
+When a consumer runs `skf install` in a project, SKF creates or modifies exactly the following on-disk paths. Paths outside this list are not touched (no surprise edits to project root). Renaming any path, changing a path's semantics, or removing a path from this list is breaking.
+
+- `_bmad/skf/` — SKF module files.
+- `_bmad/_config/skf-manifest.yaml` — install manifest. Format per `tools/cli/lib/manifest.js`; covered top-level keys: `version`, `installed_at`, `action`, `module`, `skf_folder`, `ides`, `skills_output_folder`, `forge_data_folder`, `directories[]`, `files.{skf,sidecar,ide_skills,learning,output}`. Downstream tooling that introspects an SKF install (e.g. to detect installed IDEs or list installed skills) reads this file; its top-level shape is the v1.0.0 contract.
+- `_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar/` — Ferris agent runtime state directory. The directory's existence and location are covered; the internal file structure and schemas inside are `@internal` (stable as a runtime contract between SKF versions during the life of one install, but not a downstream-consumable API).
+- `_skf-learn/` — optional learning material; created only when the user opts into learning at install time.
+- `{skills_output_folder}/` — default `skills/`, configurable via install prompt and persisted to `skf-manifest.yaml`.
+- `{forge_data_folder}/` — default `forge-data/`, configurable via install prompt and persisted to `skf-manifest.yaml`.
+- **Per-IDE skill directories.** SKF installs skills to each configured IDE's conventional skills directory. The IDE → directory mapping is enumerated in `tools/cli/lib/platform-codes.yaml` under `platforms.<ide>.installer.target_dir`; that file is the source of truth and is itself part of the v1.0.0 contract. Specifically: renaming `platforms.*` or `installer.target_dir` is breaking; removing a platform's `target_dir` is breaking for consumers of that IDE; adding a new platform entry is additive (minor).
+
+### User-Facing Command Output Formats
+
+The v1.0.0 contract commits to the **information each command outputs** and to **exit-code semantics**, not to exact human-readable wording, ANSI color choices, or console layout. Downstream tooling that needs structured introspection of an install should read `_bmad/_config/skf-manifest.yaml` directly (see [§ Installation Layout](#installation-layout)), which IS committed verbatim.
+
+- **`skf status`** SHALL convey: installation-present boolean, installed version string, forge-tier name (or a "not detected" equivalent), configured IDE list, workflow-skill count, sidecar state, and output-folder paths. Exit `0` on success; `1` on unhandled failure.
+- **`skf install`** SHALL exit `0` on successful install and on user-cancel, `1` on unhandled failure. The success path SHALL emit a message referencing the SKF folder path created in the project.
+- **`skf update`** SHALL exit `0` on successful update; `1` on unhandled failure. Update preserves existing config and sidecar state.
+- **`skf uninstall`** SHALL exit `0` on successful removal, on "nothing to do" (no prior install detected), and on user-declined confirmation. Exit `1` on unhandled failure.
+
+Explicit non-commitments: ANSI color choices, exact prose wording, precise layout of tables or lists, and i18n prose variations are `@internal` and may change in minor releases.
+
+## @internal — Not Covered by SemVer
+
+The following surfaces are explicitly excluded from the v1.0.0 contract. Changes to any of these may land in any release — patch, minor, or major — without constituting a "breaking change" from a SemVer perspective. Downstream consumers SHOULD NOT pin against these surfaces.
+
+- **`tools/cli/lib/*` internal helper modules** — implementation detail of the CLI; not a library. Any refactor that preserves the observable CLI surface and install layout above is allowed.
+- **Internal structure of workflow step files under `src/skf-*/steps-c/`** — the workflow authoring format is an SKF-internal authoring surface; step numbering, file names, and prose can change.
+- **`_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar/*.yaml` file schemas** — Ferris sidecar state. Stable as a runtime contract between SKF versions during one install's lifetime (so `skf update` works), but not a downstream-consumable schema.
+- **Ferris agent persona prose and menu wording** — the in-product agent persona can be rephrased or restructured at any time.
+- **Exact chalk styling / ANSI color choices in command output** — stylistic; see the output-format bucket above for what IS committed.
+- **Internal logic of the `Installer` class in `tools/cli/lib/installer.js`** — private implementation; what's covered is the observable install layout, not how the files get there.
+- **Workflow `{communication_language}` / `{document_output_language}` template substitution** — an authoring convenience for workflow prose; the mechanism is internal.
+- **Knowledge-base contents under `src/knowledge/` and `src/skf-*/references/`** — evolving reference material, not a schema.
+- **Test fixtures and test file layout under `test/`** — test organization is internal.
+- **Build and docs tooling (`tools/build-docs.js`, `website/`)** — internal build pipeline for the Starlight docs site; not a consumer surface.
+- **`_bmad-output/` planning-artifact layout** — gitignored and never distributed; not shipped in the npm package.
+
+## How to Use This Contract
+
+Pin SKF as a dependency with caret-range major-version pinning to get minor and patch updates automatically:
+
+```json
+"bmad-module-skill-forge": "^1.0.0"
+```
+
+The caret range covers additive changes and internal refactors on covered surfaces, and any change on `@internal` surfaces. If you find that an SKF release broke a covered surface listed in this document, please open an issue at the SKF issue tracker citing the relevant section — it is either a bug in SKF that needs reverting or a missed major-version bump.
+
+If a surface you depend on is NOT explicitly enumerated in [§ Covered Surfaces](#covered-surfaces), treat it as `@internal` — if in doubt, it is `@internal`. If you believe a surface should be promoted from `@internal` to covered, open an issue with a use-case description; promotion is additive and can land in a minor release.
+
+## Changes to This Contract
+
+Shrinking the covered surface (removing a commitment) is a breaking change and requires a major-version bump. Expanding the covered surface (promoting an `@internal` surface to covered, or adding a new commitment) is additive and ships in a minor release. This document is versioned alongside the package; historical versions are visible via `git log docs/STABILITY.md`.
+
+## References
+
+- [agentskills.io spec](https://github.com/agentskills/agentskills) — external canonical source for the `SKILL.md` frontmatter grammar.
+- [Semantic Versioning 2.0.0](https://semver.org/) — the SemVer semantics this contract commits to.
+- [CHANGELOG.md](../CHANGELOG.md) — release-by-release history; breaking changes are called out there.
+- [docs/RELEASING.md](RELEASING.md) — maintainer reference for the release pipeline (companion document: this file is about what ships, `RELEASING.md` is about how).
+- `tools/cli/lib/platform-codes.yaml` — source of truth for the IDE → skills-directory mapping covered under [§ Installation Layout](#installation-layout).
+- `test/schema/agent.js` — zod schema definitions for `bmad-skill-manifest.yaml` covered under [§ Skill Manifest & Frontmatter Contract](#skill-manifest--frontmatter-contract).
```
=== DIFF END ===

Return the findings list only. No preamble, no summary of the diff, no "overall assessment."

content.text:
- **Self-contradiction on CLI flag coverage** — category: contradiction — The doc asserts `-V, --version` and `-h, --help` are covered top-level options, then claims "Per-subcommand flags: none are currently part of the v1.0.0 surface. Each subcommand today accepts no flags." Help/version are commander-provided on subcommands too by default, and even at the top level the wording flatly conflicts with the adjacent "no flags" claim without reconciling it. Evidence: `"Top-level options: -V, --version ... and -h, --help ..."` vs. `"Per-subcommand flags: none are currently part of the v1.0.0 surface."`

- **Exit-code contract is internally inconsistent** — category: contradiction — The CLI bucket states `0` on success and `1` on unhandled failure and calls "intermediate exit codes" out-of-scope, but the output-format bucket later commits `skf uninstall` to exit `0` on user-declined confirmation and `skf install` to exit `0` on user-cancel. That is a semantic commitment, not a raw success/failure commitment, and it collides with "intermediate exit codes are not part of the contract." Evidence: `"0 on success ... 1 on unhandled failure. Intermediate exit codes are not part of the contract."` vs. `"skf uninstall SHALL exit 0 on successful removal, on 'nothing to do' ... and on user-declined confirmation."`

- **"Status: DRAFT" undermines the entire contract** — category: unsupported claim — The doc simultaneously calls itself "the public API contract for `bmad-module-skill-forge` at v1.0.0" and flags itself as DRAFT with a review window that locks "at v1.0.0 cut." Shipping/linking a DRAFT as though it were normative is a red flag; the README addition presents it as authoritative with no draft caveat. Evidence: `"**Status:** DRAFT — locks at v1.0.0 cut (Story 5.1 pre-flight audit). Review-window open until then."` and README: `"The v1.0.0 public API contract is documented in [docs/STABILITY.md](docs/STABILITY.md)."`

- **`package.json main` claim is suspicious** — category: suspicious assertion — The doc says `package.json`'s `main` field points at `tools/cli/skf-cli.js` and hand-waves this as "standard npm resolution for bin-like packages." That's not a standard idiom — `main` is for library entry points, `bin` is for CLIs. Pointing `main` at a file with side-effectful `program.parse(process.argv)` at import time is a real bug the doc casually normalizes rather than flags. Evidence: `"Though package.json's main field points at this file (standard npm resolution for bin-like packages), it has no programmatic export surface."`

- **Programmatic "no export" claim is not verifiable from the diff and is self-contradictory in effect** — category: logic — The text promises that `require('bmad-module-skill-forge')` runs `program.parse(process.argv)` "as top-level side effects," yet simultaneously asserts the file has "no programmatic export surface" and that embedding is `@internal`. Executing side effects on require IS a programmatic surface — a very hostile one. Calling it "not a supported usage" does not erase the behavior from the contract. Evidence: `"A require('bmad-module-skill-forge') from Node runs the CLI parser against the caller's process.argv — not a supported usage."`

- **Broken/unverifiable external link to agentskills.io spec** — category: broken link — The doc references `https://github.com/agentskills/agentskills` as the canonical spec twice and claims SKF tracks upstream. No version/commit pin is given, so the "contract" floats against an unpinned third-party repo. If upstream renames a field tomorrow, SKF's v1.0.0 contract silently changes. Evidence: `"SKF-shipped SKILL.md files conform to the agentskills.io minimal frontmatter spec"` and `"[agentskills.io spec](https://github.com/agentskills/agentskills) — external canonical source"`

- **Embedded zod schema will drift from the source of truth** — category: logic / maintenance hazard — The doc inlines a copy of the zod schema from `test/schema/agent.js` and declares it the contract. The inlined copy is guaranteed to drift as the file evolves unless a test enforces parity, and none is mentioned. Worse, the source of truth is a file under `test/` — but the doc elsewhere declares "Test fixtures and test file layout under `test/`" as `@internal`. Evidence: `"The agent-type skill manifest shape SKF uses is validated by the zod schema at test/schema/agent.js:"` with inlined code, vs. `"Test fixtures and test file layout under test/ — test organization is internal."`

- **Contradiction: test/ is internal but test/schema/agent.js is the contract** — category: contradiction — Directly related to the above: the doc simultaneously cites `test/schema/agent.js` as a covered surface's source of truth AND lists `test/` wholesale as `@internal`. Pick one. Evidence: `"test/schema/agent.js — zod schema definitions ... covered under § Skill Manifest & Frontmatter Contract."` vs. `"Test fixtures and test file layout under test/ — test organization is internal."`

- **`_bmad-output/` claim is not obviously true** — category: suspicious assertion — The doc asserts `_bmad-output/` is "gitignored and never distributed; not shipped in the npm package." Two separate claims, both asserted without reference to `.gitignore` or `.npmignore`/`files` field. A reader has no way to verify either from the diff. Evidence: `"_bmad-output/ planning-artifact layout — gitignored and never distributed; not shipped in the npm package."`

- **"Renaming or removing any `skf:*` script is breaking" but `release:*` scripts are excluded by fiat** — category: unsupported claim — The parenthetical says `release:*` scripts are "internal-to-maintainers and are being retired across Epic 4 and Epic 6." Epic references are internal planning artifacts meaningless to a downstream reader, and the claim that any npm script beginning with `release:` is definitionally not part of the public surface is asserted, not argued. `npm run <anything>` is publicly invocable from an installed package. Evidence: `"(Note: release:* scripts are NOT part of this contract — they are internal-to-maintainers and are being retired across Epic 4 and Epic 6.)"`

- **Forge-sidecar directory: "existence and location covered" but internals `@internal`** — category: logic — The doc promises `_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar/` exists and lives where it lives, but says the internal files are `@internal` while also being "stable as a runtime contract between SKF versions during the life of one install." That is either (a) a real compatibility guarantee, in which case it's part of the contract, or (b) not a guarantee, in which case `skf update` has no way to promise state preservation. Pick one. Evidence: `"Ferris sidecar state. Stable as a runtime contract between SKF versions during one install's lifetime (so skf update works), but not a downstream-consumable schema."`

- **Install-layout list is simultaneously closed and open-ended** — category: contradiction — "SKF creates or modifies exactly the following on-disk paths. Paths outside this list are not touched (no surprise edits to project root)." Then the list includes `{skills_output_folder}/` (default `skills/`, user-configurable) and `{forge_data_folder}/` (default `forge-data/`, user-configurable). A user-configurable path is by definition not "exactly" anything — the contract is the mechanism, not the path. The wording oversells determinism. Evidence: `"SKF creates or modifies exactly the following on-disk paths. Paths outside this list are not touched"` vs. `"{skills_output_folder}/ — default skills/, configurable via install prompt"`

- **`platform-codes.yaml` is covered, but internal-YAML structural changes are silently in-scope** — category: logic — The doc commits `platforms.*` and `installer.target_dir` as covered keys in `tools/cli/lib/platform-codes.yaml`, then says "the file is itself part of the v1.0.0 contract." The entire file is claimed as a contract surface, yet only two key paths are called out. What about every other key? Readers cannot tell what's covered vs. incidental. Evidence: `"that file is the source of truth and is itself part of the v1.0.0 contract. Specifically: renaming platforms.* or installer.target_dir is breaking"`

- **SHALL vs MAY/SHOULD usage is inconsistent with RFC 2119 conventions** — category: formatting / prose — The doc mixes RFC-2119-style "SHALL" keywords with informal prose ("SHOULD NOT pin," "SHOULD read," "may change") without a normative-keyword preamble. For a document that purports to be a contract, this is sloppy. Evidence: `"skf status SHALL convey ..."` and `"Downstream tooling that needs structured introspection ... should read _bmad/_config/skf-manifest.yaml directly"` and `"Downstream consumers SHOULD NOT pin against these surfaces."`

- **"Additive changes to covered surfaces are minor bumps"** — category: unsupported claim — The opening paragraph says additive changes to covered surfaces are minor bumps. Then "Adding a new optional field is additive" to the strict zod schema. A `.strict()` schema rejects unknown keys — so a consumer writing manifests against v1.0.0 that adds a key later introduced by SKF would have been rejected by v1.0.0 and accepted by v1.1.0. That is not uncontroversially "additive" from the consumer-authoring angle; it silently changes the set of documents that round-trip. Evidence: `"The schema is strict — unknown keys are rejected. ... Adding a new optional field is additive."`

- **`module` field is "always `skf`" — dead weight in the contract** — category: logic — If the `module` field is always `skf` for SKF-authored manifests, why is it in the schema as a required-by-consumer field? Either SKF is documenting a field it injects (in which case it should say so, not claim it's a required input), or consumers must supply `module: skf` verbatim (in which case changing the string is breaking and should be called out). Evidence: `"module: createNonEmptyString('module'), // required"` and `"The module field is always skf for SKF-authored manifests."`

- **Anchor/link to `#internal--not-covered-by-semver` is suspicious** — category: broken link — The in-doc reference uses anchor `#internal--not-covered-by-semver` but the heading is `## @internal — Not Covered by SemVer`. Different Markdown renderers slugify `@` and em-dashes inconsistently; this anchor is brittle and may 404 on common renderers (Starlight/MDX vs. GitHub's gfm slugger). No smoke test of the rendered anchor is evident. Evidence: `"(see [§ @internal — Not Covered by SemVer](#internal--not-covered-by-semver))"`

- **README addition buries the contract under "Release Process"** — category: formatting — The README hunk places "Versioning & Stability" as a three-line section immediately above "Release Process," with nothing but a single sentence and a link. This undersells a document the doc itself describes as "the public API contract." Evidence: `"## Versioning & Stability\n\nThe v1.0.0 public API contract is documented in [docs/STABILITY.md](docs/STABILITY.md)."`

- **"Open an issue at the SKF issue tracker" without a URL** — category: broken reference — For a contract document intended for downstream consumers, telling them to "open an issue at the SKF issue tracker" without a link is lazy. Evidence: `"please open an issue at the SKF issue tracker citing the relevant section"`

- **"Historical versions are visible via `git log docs/STABILITY.md`"** — category: suspicious assertion — This assumes the consumer has a git checkout. A user who installed via npm does not, and pointing them at git log is not a substitute for a dated CHANGELOG entry or section-level version markers inside the document itself. Evidence: `"This document is versioned alongside the package; historical versions are visible via git log docs/STABILITY.md."`

- **Review window "locks at v1.0.0 cut" — no cut date, no process** — category: unsupported claim — The DRAFT banner says the doc "locks at v1.0.0 cut (Story 5.1 pre-flight audit)." No date. No criteria for who signs off. No reference to where Story 5.1 lives for an outside reader. This is internal jargon leaking into a purportedly-public document. Evidence: `"**Status:** DRAFT — locks at v1.0.0 cut (Story 5.1 pre-flight audit). Review-window open until then."`

- **"Trigger phrase" requirement is under-specified** — category: unsupported claim — The doc asserts the `description` field "terminates with an explicit trigger phrase" and points at "live examples" in the repo. A contract field cannot be defined by "go look at some files." If this is normative, the grammar belongs in the doc or an explicit pointer to the agentskills.io section that defines it. Evidence: `"description (one-line natural-language summary that terminates with an explicit trigger phrase — see the src/skf-*/SKILL.md files in this repo for live examples)"`

- **Caret-range advice is misleading for pre-1.0 consumers** — category: logic — The doc recommends `"^1.0.0"` pinning but makes no mention that SKF is not yet at v1.0.0 at the time this DRAFT ships. A consumer reading this today cannot pin `^1.0.0` against a published v1.0.0 because the contract itself says it "locks at v1.0.0 cut." Evidence: `"Pin SKF as a dependency with caret-range major-version pinning ... \"bmad-module-skill-forge\": \"^1.0.0\""`

- **No mention of Node.js version support as part of the contract** — category: missing commitment — A public API contract that covers a CLI, script aliases, and install behavior but does not name a supported Node.js version range is incomplete. A minor Node bump could break consumers silently, and the doc is silent on whether that is breaking. Evidence: entire doc — no `engines`, `node`, or runtime-version language present.

- **No mention of supported operating systems** — category: missing commitment — The install layout references on-disk paths and IDE-specific target directories; the contract is silent on Windows vs. POSIX path handling. For a CLI shipping to npm, this is a conspicuous omission. Evidence: entire doc — no `platform`, `windows`, `posix` language present.

- **Contract covers `skf install` "user-cancel" but never defines user-cancel** — category: unsupported claim — "0 on success (including user-cancel on `install` ...)." Nowhere does the doc state what counts as a user-cancel (Ctrl-C? declining a prompt? quitting mid-install and leaving partial state?). For a committed exit-code semantic, this is underspecified. Evidence: `"0 on success (including user-cancel on install and 'nothing to do' paths on uninstall)"`

- **"Update preserves existing config and sidecar state"** — category: unsupported claim — A single sentence promises a nontrivial invariant (config + sidecar preservation across updates) with no reference to test coverage, no definition of "state," and the sidecar schemas are elsewhere labeled `@internal`. Preservation of an `@internal` schema is not meaningfully promisable without at least a round-trip guarantee. Evidence: `"skf update SHALL exit 0 on successful update; 1 on unhandled failure. Update preserves existing config and sidecar state."`

# Step 3: Triage

## RULES

- YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT in your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`
- Be precise. When uncertain between categories, prefer the more conservative classification.

## INSTRUCTIONS

1. **Normalize** findings into a common format. Expected input formats:
   - Adversarial (Blind Hunter): markdown list of descriptions
   - Edge Case Hunter: JSON array with `location`, `trigger_condition`, `guard_snippet`, `potential_consequence` fields
   - Acceptance Auditor: markdown list with title, AC/constraint reference, and evidence

   If a layer's output does not match its expected format, attempt best-effort parsing. Note any parsing issues for the user.

   Convert all to a unified list where each finding has:
   - `id` -- sequential integer
   - `source` -- `blind`, `edge`, `auditor`, or merged sources (e.g., `blind+edge`)
   - `title` -- one-line summary
   - `detail` -- full description
   - `location` -- file and line reference (if available)

2. **Deduplicate.** If two or more findings describe the same issue, merge them into one:
   - Use the most specific finding as the base (prefer edge-case JSON with location over adversarial prose).
   - Append any unique detail, reasoning, or location references from the other finding(s) into the surviving `detail` field.
   - Set `source` to the merged sources (e.g., `blind+edge`).

3. **Classify** each finding into exactly one bucket:
   - **decision_needed** -- There is an ambiguous choice that requires human input. The code cannot be correctly patched without knowing the user's intent. Only possible if `{review_mode}` = `"full"`.
   - **patch** -- Code issue that is fixable without human input. The correct fix is unambiguous.
   - **defer** -- Pre-existing issue not caused by the current change. Real but not actionable now.
   - **dismiss** -- Noise, false positive, or handled elsewhere.

   If `{review_mode}` = `"no-spec"` and a finding would otherwise be `decision_needed`, reclassify it as `patch` (if the fix is unambiguous) or `defer` (if not).

4. **Drop** all `dismiss` findings. Record the dismiss count for the summary.

5. If `{failed_layers}` is non-empty, report which layers failed before announcing results. If zero findings remain after dropping dismissed AND `{failed_layers}` is non-empty, warn the user that the review may be incomplete rather than announcing a clean review.

6. If zero findings remain after triage (all rejected or none raised): state "✅ Clean review — all layers passed." (Step 3 already warned if any review layers failed via `{failed_layers}`.)


## NEXT

Read fully and follow `./step-04-present.md`

Write all findings in this order: 22 23 1. **`decision-needed`** findings (unchecked): 24 `- [ ] [Review][Decision] — <Detail>` 25 26 2. **`patch`** findings (unchecked): 27 `- [ ] [Review][Patch] <Title> [<file>:<line>]` 28 29 3. **`defer`** findings (checked off, marked deferred): 30 `- [x] [Review][Defer] <Title> [<file>:<line>] — deferred, pre-existing` 31 32 Also append each `defer` finding to `{deferred_work_file}` under a heading `## Deferred from: code review ({date})`. If `{spec_file}` is set, include its basename in the heading (e.g., `code review of story-3.3 (2026-03-18)`). One bullet per finding with description. 33 34 ### 3. Present summary 35 36 Announce what was written: 37 38 > **Code review complete.** <D> `decision-needed`, <P> `patch`, <W> `defer`, <R> dismissed as noise. 39 40 If `{spec_file}` is set, add: `Findings written to the review findings section in {spec_file}.` 41 Otherwise add: `Findings are listed above. No story file was provided, so nothing was persisted.` 42 43 ### 4. Resolve decision-needed findings 44 45 If `decision_needed` findings exist, present each one with its detail and the options available. The user must decide — the correct fix is ambiguous without their input. Walk through each finding (or batch related ones) and get the user's call. Once resolved, each becomes a `patch`, `defer`, or is dismissed. 46 47 If the user chooses to defer, ask: Quick one-line reason for deferring this item? (helps future reviews): — then append that reason to both the story file bullet and the `{deferred_work_file}` entry. 48 49 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 50 51 ### 5. Handle `patch` findings 52 53 If `patch` findings exist (including any resolved from step 4), HALT. Ask the user: 54 55 If `{spec_file}` is set, present all three options (if >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0): 56 57 > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** 58 > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) 59 > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now 60 > 2. **Leave as action items** — they are already in the story file 61 > 3. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding 62 63 If `{spec_file}` is **not** set, present only options 1 and 3 (omit option 2 — findings were not written to a file). If >3 `patch` findings exist, also show option 0: 64 65 > **How would you like to handle the <Z> `patch` findings?** 66 > 0. **Batch-apply all** — automatically fix every non-controversial patch (recommended when there are many) 67 > 1. **Fix them automatically** — I will apply fixes now 68 > 2. **Walk through each** — let me show details before deciding 69 70 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 71 72 - **Option 0** (only when >3 findings): Apply all non-controversial patches without per-finding confirmation. Skip any finding that requires judgment. Present a summary of changes made and any skipped findings. 73 - **Option 1**: Apply each fix. After all patches are applied, present a summary of changes made. If `{spec_file}` is set, check off the items in the story file. 74 - **Option 2** (only when `{spec_file}` is set): Done — findings are already written to the story. 75 - **Walk through each**: Present each finding with full detail, diff context, and suggested fix. After walkthrough, re-offer the applicable options above. 76 77 **HALT** — I am waiting for your numbered choice. Reply with only the number (or "0" for batch). Do not proceed until you select an option. 78 79 **✅ Code review actions complete** 80 81 - Decision-needed resolved: <D> 82 - Patches handled: <P> 83 - Deferred: <W> 84 - Dismissed: <R> 85 86 ### 6. Update story status and sync sprint tracking 87 88 Skip this section if `{spec_file}` is not set. 89 90 #### Determine new status based on review outcome 91 92 - If all `decision-needed` and `patch` findings were resolved (fixed or dismissed) AND no unresolved HIGH/MEDIUM issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `done`. Update the story file Status section to `done`. 93 - If `patch` findings were left as action items, or unresolved issues remain: set `{new_status}` = `in-progress`. Update the story file Status section to `in-progress`. 94 95 Save the story file. 96 97 #### Sync sprint-status.yaml 98 99 If `{story_key}` is not set, skip this subsection and note that sprint status was not synced because no story key was available. 100 101 If `{sprint_status}` file exists: 102 103 1. Load the FULL `{sprint_status}` file. 104 2. Find the `development_status` entry matching `{story_key}`. 105 3. If found: update `development_status[{story_key}]` to `{new_status}`. Update `last_updated` to current date. Save the file, preserving ALL comments and structure including STATUS DEFINITIONS. 106 4. If `{story_key}` not found in sprint status: warn the user that the story file was updated but sprint-status sync failed. 107 108 If `{sprint_status}` file does not exist, note that story status was updated in the story file only. 109 110 #### Completion summary 111 112 > **Review Complete!** 113 > 114 > **Story Status:** `{new_status}` 115 > **Issues Fixed:** <fixed_count> 116 > **Action Items Created:** <action_count> 117 > **Deferred:** <W> 118 > **Dismissed:** <R> 119 120 ### 7. Next steps 121 122 Present the user with follow-up options: 123 124 > **What would you like to do next?** 125 > 1. **Start the next story** — run `dev-story` to pick up the next `ready-for-dev` story 126 > 2. **Re-run code review** — address findings and review again 127 > 3. **Done** — end the workflow 128 129 **HALT** — I am waiting for your choice. Do not proceed until the user selects an option. 130

content.content:
- **Baseline.json capture procedure unspecified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:52-65`. Restore is aspirational if no baseline has been captured; needs a companion capture command and guidance on where baselines live. Story 4.1 rollback playbook is the natural home.
- **Site-nav discoverability of `docs/RELEASING.md` (Starlight/Astro sidebar) unverified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:1-4`. Frontmatter is Starlight-compatible but no sidebar entry was audited; if the docs site is built with Starlight, readers arriving at the site may not find the page without a direct URL. Confirm docs-site build config before acting.

## Deferred from: code review of 1-3-register-npm-trusted-publisher-for-release-yaml-workflow (2026-04-20)

- **Story 3.3 precondition — neutralize `publish.yaml` tag-push trigger atomically with `release.yaml` merge to main** — `.github/workflows/publish.yaml:10,37` has `id-token: write` and `npm publish --provenance` with `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN` wired. Until Story 6.1 deletes it, a stray `v*` tag push will fire `publish.yaml` alongside (or instead of) `release.yaml`, producing publishes attributed to the wrong workflow in the provenance record — an audit-trail inconsistency, not a hard failure (token auth bypasses the TP matcher). When Story 3.3 merges `release.yaml` to main, in the same PR remove `publish.yaml`'s `v*` tag trigger (leave `workflow_dispatch` only as an emergency hatch) so the release path is single-rooted. Resolved as defer in Story 1.3 code review D2 to avoid scope creep into a doc-only story.
- **Story 3.1 precondition — workflow filename MUST be `release.yaml` (not `.yml`)** — the npm Trusted Publisher registered 2026-04-20 binds on filename `release.yaml` exactly. A `.yml` typo in Story 3.1 produces an opaque 404 on Story 3.2's alpha cut with no recovery path short of deleting-and-re-adding the TP. Re-read `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher` before authoring the workflow. Enforcement is prose-only by design (Story 1.3 code review D1, resolved as defer): a stub file on main was rejected as overkill for a low-probability risk that is triple-guarded (story Dev Notes, RELEASING.md rename-coupling note, Story 3.2 alpha cut as live validator).
- **Solo-maintainer self-review weakness inherited by TP section** — `docs/RELEASING.md § Release Environment`. New `## npm Trusted Publisher` section relies on the "required reviewer" gate, which the adjacent Release Environment section admits is self-approved (`prevent_self_review: false`). The TP section does not re-acknowledge this — an auditor reading only the new section will overestimate the control. Belongs with the Story 1.2 self-review gap (already captured there).
- **Rollback Playbook heading is empty / TOC lands on no content** — `docs/RELEASING.md § Rollback Playbook`. Bare `## Rollback Playbook` heading follows the `<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->` placeholder. Readers hitting the TOC click into an empty section until Story 4.1 lands. Story 4.1's natural territory.
- **`NPM_TOKEN` rotation/deletion before Story 6.3 has no documented fallback** — `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher`. Section names the token as "last-resort token-based publish" if OIDC breaks, but does not cover the inverse failure: token rotated/revoked during an OIDC-broken window leaves both paths down. Incident-response design belongs in Story 6.3's removal plan.
- **No CI/test asserts the TP registration's shape matches repo reality** — docs-only change; no guard. Drift between the npm-side TP and the `release.yaml`/`release` env it binds to is only caught at publish time (Story 3.2 onward). A CI job that reads the RELEASING.md table and verifies (a) `.github/workflows/release.yaml` exists, (b) environment `release` exists, (c) `package.json` name matches, would turn prose into an enforced gate. Standalone story once Story 3.x lands.

## Deferred from: code review of 2-1-add-conventional-changelog-cli-as-a-devdependency (2026-04-20)

- **CI-log deprecation-warning noise persists until Story 3.1 tooling decision** — `.github/workflows/*.yaml` via `npm ci`. Every CI run prints `npm warn deprecated conventional-changelog-cli@5.0.0: This package is no longer maintained.` Not a failure, but permanent log noise until the "Story 3.1 tooling decision" (below) resolves one way or the other. Acknowledged so future reviewers don't miss other deprecation notices amongst the repeated line.
- **Latent devDep mirror-entry drift — class-of-bug guard not in CI** — `package.json` / `package-lock.json`. Story 2.1's install silently re-added the `packages[""].devDependencies.zod` mirror entry that had been missing from the lockfile's root devDep listing. Resolved version unchanged, but the class of bug ("`package.json` says X, lockfile mirror is missing, install normalizes") is now a known defect-class with 22 other devDeps unchecked. Add a one-line CI assertion: every key in `package.json.devDependencies` has a mirror in `package-lock.json.packages[""].devDependencies`. Not blocking for Story 2.2 / 3.1; lightweight hygiene win.
- **`packageManager` field not set — dev/CI npm version divergence widens with the new subtree** — `package.json`. Dev Agent Record records local `node v24.12.0 / npm 11.6.2`; `.nvmrc` pins `22` for CI. The 46-package subtree added in Story 2.1 widens the surface on which an npm 11 vs npm 10 lockfile-emission divergence could surface. Pin via `"packageManager": "npm@X.Y.Z"` and add `engines.npm`. Pre-existing gap, not introduced by Story 2.1, but the upside grows with every new devDep.
- **No `files[]` whitelist on `package.json`; tarball contents rely solely on `.npmignore`** — `package.json`. With the new 46-package `conventional-changelog-cli` subtree in dev install, a future `.npmignore` edit could inadvertently ship the subtree to registry consumers. Add a `npm pack --dry-run` CI assertion or switch to explicit `files[]`. Most naturally folded into Story 3.1's `release.yaml` pre-publish step.
- **No Windows-matrix smoke test for `conventional-changelog-cli` bin resolution** — `.github/workflows/quality.yaml`. Matrix runs `windows-latest`; `tempfile@5.0.0` in the new transitive closure is unverified on Windows path/EOL handling. Add `npx --no-install conventional-changelog-cli --help` as a Windows-cell smoke step before Story 3.1 relies on the tool in CI, so a Windows-only resolution failure surfaces pre-release rather than at publish time.

## Deferred from: implementation of 2-1-add-conventional-changelog-cli-as-a-devdependency (2026-04-20)

- **Story 3.1 tooling decision — `conventional-changelog-cli@5` is marked deprecated by upstream** — `package.json` devDependencies. Install surfaced `npm warn deprecated conventional-changelog-cli@5.0.0: This package is no longer maintained. Please use the conventional-changelog package instead.` The tool remains functional (Story 2.1 AC #4/#5 verified both `--help` and `-p conventionalcommits` resolve cleanly via `--no-install`), so Story 2.1 proceeded per its literal AC #1 (`"conventional-changelog-cli": "^5.0.0"`). Before Story 3.1 embeds `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -i CHANGELOG.md -s` in `release.yaml`, its create-story / design phase should revisit the tooling choice:  (a) stay on the deprecated-but-working `conventional-changelog-cli@^5.0.0` CLI (status-quo; tolerates the upstream deprecation signal), (b) migrate to the suggested `conventional-changelog` package (different CLI surface; story 2.2's `-r 0` invocation shape would need revalidating), or (c) the research doc's rejected-but-still-viable alternative `git-cliff` (one-binary, Rust; different preset semantics). Decision should weigh release-workflow reliability (which is the whole point of Epic 3) against adopting an actively-maintained tool before it atrophies further. Flag to Story 3.1 planner, not a blocker for Story 2.2 (seeding works on v5).

## Deferred from: code review of 2-2-seed-initial-changelog-md-from-existing-git-tags (2026-04-21)

- **Annotate dangling `Revert "0.2.0"` entry in v0.3.0 Reverts section** — `CHANGELOG.md:257`. The seeded v0.3.0 section includes `Revert "0.2.0"` (a historical revert of a never-published 0.2.0 tag). Reader following the reference will find no 0.2.0 section because v0.3.0 is the first seeded version. Cosmetic; Story 5.1's pre-flight audit (which will hand-edit v1.0.0 prose into the top of the file) is the natural place to add a `(pre-v0.3.0 tag, never published)` parenthetical. Not blocking for Story 2.2's seed.
- **No CI link-check validates CHANGELOG.md compare URLs and issue URLs** — `CHANGELOG.md`. Story 2.2's hand-review caught four bogus `closes [#<slug>]` noise lines AC #5 required to be stripped (F1 patch applied in this review), but nothing prevents regression on future `release.yaml` append-mode writes. A `lychee CHANGELOG.md` or `markdown-link-check` CI step scoped to the changelog would catch: (a) bogus non-numeric issue refs from future preset mis-classification, (b) broken compare URLs if the repo is renamed, (c) dangling section anchors. Most naturally folded into Story 3.1's `release.yaml` quality gate or Story 5.1's pre-flight audit.
- **Story 3.1 append-mode dry-run pre-validation against the seeded file** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml` (not yet authored). Story 3.1's `release.yaml` will run `conventional-changelog-cli -i CHANGELOG.md -s -r 1` against the seed. Behavior when no `[Unreleased]` anchor exists is preset-specific and currently unverified — the preset may insert above v0.10.0, produce a malformed top heading, or refuse. A one-shot local dry-run before Story 3.1 lands would catch any seam mismatch. Story 3.2's alpha-cut is the live validator per Dev Notes § "Coupling with past, current, and future stories", so this is belt-and-braces, not strictly required.
- **`[Unreleased]` anchor decision for Story 3.1 append mode** — `CHANGELOG.md:1-5`. Story 2.2's seed did not emit `## [Unreleased]` (AC #3 permits absence). Story 3.1's `release.yaml` design must decide whether to (a) hand-insert `[Unreleased]` as part of the first `release.yaml` run, (b) rely on `conventional-changelog-cli`'s default prepend-above-topmost-version behavior, or (c) switch tool/preset if neither works cleanly. Deferred from Story 2.2 code review 2026-04-21 per Dev Agent Record § Review Findings Decision 2.

toolUseResult.stdout:
- **Baseline.json capture procedure unspecified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:52-65`. Restore is aspirational if no baseline has been captured; needs a companion capture command and guidance on where baselines live. Story 4.1 rollback playbook is the natural home.
- **Site-nav discoverability of `docs/RELEASING.md` (Starlight/Astro sidebar) unverified** — `docs/RELEASING.md:1-4`. Frontmatter is Starlight-compatible but no sidebar entry was audited; if the docs site is built with Starlight, readers arriving at the site may not find the page without a direct URL. Confirm docs-site build config before acting.

## Deferred from: code review of 1-3-register-npm-trusted-publisher-for-release-yaml-workflow (2026-04-20)

- **Story 3.3 precondition — neutralize `publish.yaml` tag-push trigger atomically with `release.yaml` merge to main** — `.github/workflows/publish.yaml:10,37` has `id-token: write` and `npm publish --provenance` with `NODE_AUTH_TOKEN` wired. Until Story 6.1 deletes it, a stray `v*` tag push will fire `publish.yaml` alongside (or instead of) `release.yaml`, producing publishes attributed to the wrong workflow in the provenance record — an audit-trail inconsistency, not a hard failure (token auth bypasses the TP matcher). When Story 3.3 merges `release.yaml` to main, in the same PR remove `publish.yaml`'s `v*` tag trigger (leave `workflow_dispatch` only as an emergency hatch) so the release path is single-rooted. Resolved as defer in Story 1.3 code review D2 to avoid scope creep into a doc-only story.
- **Story 3.1 precondition — workflow filename MUST be `release.yaml` (not `.yml`)** — the npm Trusted Publisher registered 2026-04-20 binds on filename `release.yaml` exactly. A `.yml` typo in Story 3.1 produces an opaque 404 on Story 3.2's alpha cut with no recovery path short of deleting-and-re-adding the TP. Re-read `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher` before authoring the workflow. Enforcement is prose-only by design (Story 1.3 code review D1, resolved as defer): a stub file on main was rejected as overkill for a low-probability risk that is triple-guarded (story Dev Notes, RELEASING.md rename-coupling note, Story 3.2 alpha cut as live validator).
- **Solo-maintainer self-review weakness inherited by TP section** — `docs/RELEASING.md § Release Environment`. New `## npm Trusted Publisher` section relies on the "required reviewer" gate, which the adjacent Release Environment section admits is self-approved (`prevent_self_review: false`). The TP section does not re-acknowledge this — an auditor reading only the new section will overestimate the control. Belongs with the Story 1.2 self-review gap (already captured there).
- **Rollback Playbook heading is empty / TOC lands on no content** — `docs/RELEASING.md § Rollback Playbook`. Bare `## Rollback Playbook` heading follows the `<!-- Rollback Playbook — added in Story 4.1 -->` placeholder. Readers hitting the TOC click into an empty section until Story 4.1 lands. Story 4.1's natural territory.
- **`NPM_TOKEN` rotation/deletion before Story 6.3 has no documented fallback** — `docs/RELEASING.md § npm Trusted Publisher`. Section names the token as "last-resort token-based publish" if OIDC breaks, but does not cover the inverse failure: token rotated/revoked during an OIDC-broken window leaves both paths down. Incident-response design belongs in Story 6.3's removal plan.
- **No CI/test asserts the TP registration's shape matches repo reality** — docs-only change; no guard. Drift between the npm-side TP and the `release.yaml`/`release` env it binds to is only caught at publish time (Story 3.2 onward). A CI job that reads the RELEASING.md table and verifies (a) `.github/workflows/release.yaml` exists, (b) environment `release` exists, (c) `package.json` name matches, would turn prose into an enforced gate. Standalone story once Story 3.x lands.

## Deferred from: code review of 2-1-add-conventional-changelog-cli-as-a-devdependency (2026-04-20)

- **CI-log deprecation-warning noise persists until Story 3.1 tooling decision** — `.github/workflows/*.yaml` via `npm ci`. Every CI run prints `npm warn deprecated conventional-changelog-cli@5.0.0: This package is no longer maintained.` Not a failure, but permanent log noise until the "Story 3.1 tooling decision" (below) resolves one way or the other. Acknowledged so future reviewers don't miss other deprecation notices amongst the repeated line.
- **Latent devDep mirror-entry drift — class-of-bug guard not in CI** — `package.json` / `package-lock.json`. Story 2.1's install silently re-added the `packages[""].devDependencies.zod` mirror entry that had been missing from the lockfile's root devDep listing. Resolved version unchanged, but the class of bug ("`package.json` says X, lockfile mirror is missing, install normalizes") is now a known defect-class with 22 other devDeps unchecked. Add a one-line CI assertion: every key in `package.json.devDependencies` has a mirror in `package-lock.json.packages[""].devDependencies`. Not blocking for Story 2.2 / 3.1; lightweight hygiene win.
- **`packageManager` field not set — dev/CI npm version divergence widens with the new subtree** — `package.json`. Dev Agent Record records local `node v24.12.0 / npm 11.6.2`; `.nvmrc` pins `22` for CI. The 46-package subtree added in Story 2.1 widens the surface on which an npm 11 vs npm 10 lockfile-emission divergence could surface. Pin via `"packageManager": "npm@X.Y.Z"` and add `engines.npm`. Pre-existing gap, not introduced by Story 2.1, but the upside grows with every new devDep.
- **No `files[]` whitelist on `package.json`; tarball contents rely solely on `.npmignore`** — `package.json`. With the new 46-package `conventional-changelog-cli` subtree in dev install, a future `.npmignore` edit could inadvertently ship the subtree to registry consumers. Add a `npm pack --dry-run` CI assertion or switch to explicit `files[]`. Most naturally folded into Story 3.1's `release.yaml` pre-publish step.
- **No Windows-matrix smoke test for `conventional-changelog-cli` bin resolution** — `.github/workflows/quality.yaml`. Matrix runs `windows-latest`; `tempfile@5.0.0` in the new transitive closure is unverified on Windows path/EOL handling. Add `npx --no-install conventional-changelog-cli --help` as a Windows-cell smoke step before Story 3.1 relies on the tool in CI, so a Windows-only resolution failure surfaces pre-release rather than at publish time.

## Deferred from: implementation of 2-1-add-conventional-changelog-cli-as-a-devdependency (2026-04-20)

- **Story 3.1 tooling decision — `conventional-changelog-cli@5` is marked deprecated by upstream** — `package.json` devDependencies. Install surfaced `npm warn deprecated conventional-changelog-cli@5.0.0: This package is no longer maintained. Please use the conventional-changelog package instead.` The tool remains functional (Story 2.1 AC #4/#5 verified both `--help` and `-p conventionalcommits` resolve cleanly via `--no-install`), so Story 2.1 proceeded per its literal AC #1 (`"conventional-changelog-cli": "^5.0.0"`). Before Story 3.1 embeds `npx conventional-changelog-cli -p conventionalcommits -i CHANGELOG.md -s` in `release.yaml`, its create-story / design phase should revisit the tooling choice:  (a) stay on the deprecated-but-working `conventional-changelog-cli@^5.0.0` CLI (status-quo; tolerates the upstream deprecation signal), (b) migrate to the suggested `conventional-changelog` package (different CLI surface; story 2.2's `-r 0` invocation shape would need revalidating), or (c) the research doc's rejected-but-still-viable alternative `git-cliff` (one-binary, Rust; different preset semantics). Decision should weigh release-workflow reliability (which is the whole point of Epic 3) against adopting an actively-maintained tool before it atrophies further. Flag to Story 3.1 planner, not a blocker for Story 2.2 (seeding works on v5).

## Deferred from: code review of 2-2-seed-initial-changelog-md-from-existing-git-tags (2026-04-21)

- **Annotate dangling `Revert "0.2.0"` entry in v0.3.0 Reverts section** — `CHANGELOG.md:257`. The seeded v0.3.0 section includes `Revert "0.2.0"` (a historical revert of a never-published 0.2.0 tag). Reader following the reference will find no 0.2.0 section because v0.3.0 is the first seeded version. Cosmetic; Story 5.1's pre-flight audit (which will hand-edit v1.0.0 prose into the top of the file) is the natural place to add a `(pre-v0.3.0 tag, never published)` parenthetical. Not blocking for Story 2.2's seed.
- **No CI link-check validates CHANGELOG.md compare URLs and issue URLs** — `CHANGELOG.md`. Story 2.2's hand-review caught four bogus `closes [#<slug>]` noise lines AC #5 required to be stripped (F1 patch applied in this review), but nothing prevents regression on future `release.yaml` append-mode writes. A `lychee CHANGELOG.md` or `markdown-link-check` CI step scoped to the changelog would catch: (a) bogus non-numeric issue refs from future preset mis-classification, (b) broken compare URLs if the repo is renamed, (c) dangling section anchors. Most naturally folded into Story 3.1's `release.yaml` quality gate or Story 5.1's pre-flight audit.
- **Story 3.1 append-mode dry-run pre-validation against the seeded file** — `.github/workflows/release.yaml` (not yet authored). Story 3.1's `release.yaml` will run `conventional-changelog-cli -i CHANGELOG.md -s -r 1` against the seed. Behavior when no `[Unreleased]` anchor exists is preset-specific and currently unverified — the preset may insert above v0.10.0, produce a malformed top heading, or refuse. A one-shot local dry-run before Story 3.1 lands would catch any seam mismatch. Story 3.2's alpha-cut is the live validator per Dev Notes § "Coupling with past, current, and future stories", so this is belt-and-braces, not strictly required.
- **`[Unreleased]` anchor decision for Story 3.1 append mode** — `CHANGELOG.md:1-5`. Story 2.2's seed did not emit `## [Unreleased]` (AC #3 permits absence). Story 3.1's `release.yaml` design must decide whether to (a) hand-insert `[Unreleased]` as part of the first `release.yaml` run, (b) rely on `conventional-changelog-cli`'s default prepend-above-topmost-version behavior, or (c) switch tool/preset if neither works cleanly. Deferred from Story 2.2 code review 2026-04-21 per Dev Agent Record § Review Findings Decision 2.

input.new_string:
### Review Findings

Parallel adversarial review run 2026-04-21 via `bmad-code-review` skill against commit `2fc1069`. Three layers: Blind Hunter (diff-only), Edge Case Hunter (diff + project read), Acceptance Auditor (diff + spec). Acceptance Auditor returned "no AC violations found"; Blind Hunter and Edge Case Hunter surfaced contract-precision and documentation-fidelity issues. Triaged to 5 `decision-needed`, 14 `patch`, 5 `defer`, 10 `dismiss`.

**Decision-Needed (human judgment required):**

- [ ] [Review][Decision] **Npm tarball excludes referenced-as-contract files** — `docs/STABILITY.md` cites `test/schema/agent.js` (authoritative zod schema) and `../CHANGELOG.md` (relative link) as part of the v1.0.0 contract, but `.npmignore:7,22` excludes both from the published tarball. A downstream consumer reading `node_modules/bmad-module-skill-forge/docs/STABILITY.md` gets broken references. Options: (a) ship `test/schema/**` + `CHANGELOG.md` in the tarball via `files[]` whitelist or `.npmignore` exceptions; (b) replace relative refs with absolute GitHub URLs pinned to a commit/tag; (c) accept "contract is GitHub-only" and document that in STABILITY.md. Scope exceeds AC #8 doc-only envelope.
- [ ] [Review][Decision] **Starlight sidebar drift — `docs/STABILITY.md` not registered** — the new doc is reachable only by direct URL. `website/astro.config.mjs` Reference bucket has no entry for it. Completion Notes explicitly flagged this for a reviewer / Story 5.1 to decide. Fix is a 1-line config edit in `website/astro.config.mjs` but crosses AC #8's doc-only allowed-files envelope. Options: (a) fold into this story via a companion commit (AC #8 tweak), (b) defer to Story 5.1 pre-flight audit, (c) a standalone docs-site-nav story.
- [ ] [Review][Decision] **`test/` @internal vs `test/schema/agent.js` covered** — STABILITY.md § @internal lists "Test fixtures and test file layout under `test/` — test organization is internal" but § Skill Manifest & Frontmatter Contract simultaneously treats `test/schema/agent.js` as the authoritative source-of-truth. Resolve: (a) carve out `test/schema/**` as covered in the @internal bullet, (b) move the zod schema from `test/schema/` into a non-test location (e.g. `src/schemas/` — code change, out of docs-scope), (c) treat the inlined code block as the contract and demote `test/schema/agent.js` to "implementation detail that happens to match."
- [ ] [Review][Decision] **Node.js / OS version commitments missing from contract** — the public API contract commits to CLI subcommands, exit codes, install layout, and script aliases but is silent on supported Node.js engine range and OS (Windows vs POSIX). For a CLI shipping via npm this is a conspicuous omission — a minor Node bump could break consumers silently. Options: (a) add an `### Engine & Platform Compatibility` bullet referencing `package.json` `engines.node`, (b) explicitly declare "supported Node / OS are `@internal` at v1.0.0" (unusual but valid if intentional), (c) defer to Story 5.1 pre-flight audit with a carry-forward flag.
- [ ] [Review][Decision] **"Update preserves existing config and sidecar state" — unsupported runtime invariant** — STABILITY.md commits `skf update` to preserving config + sidecar state, but sidecar schemas are explicitly listed as `@internal`. Preserving an @internal schema requires a test-backed round-trip guarantee that doesn't exist. Options: (a) tighten to specific preserved fields (config keys in `skf-manifest.yaml`), (b) soften to "best-effort state preservation; breaking migrations will be flagged in CHANGELOG.md", (c) add a regression test (code change, out of docs-scope) and keep the commitment as-is, (d) remove the sentence.

**Patch (unambiguous fixes within docs-only scope):**

- [ ] [Review][Patch] `.gitignore` modification missing from Installation Layout [docs/STABILITY.md § Installation Layout] — `tools/cli/lib/installer.js` appends to `.gitignore` at install time, but STABILITY.md says "SKF creates or modifies exactly the following on-disk paths. Paths outside this list are not touched (no surprise edits to project root)" and omits `.gitignore`. Either add `.gitignore` as a bullet (with the specific appended entries) or soften "exactly" and "no surprise edits" wording.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] `skf install` success-message claim contradicts code [docs/STABILITY.md § User-Facing Command Output Formats] — STABILITY.md says the install success path "SHALL emit a message referencing the SKF folder path", but `tools/cli/lib/ui.js` `displaySuccess` receives `skfFolder` as a parameter and does not emit it. Options: (a) soften to "SHALL emit a success confirmation" (docs-only), (b) code-change to echo the path (out of docs-scope).
- [ ] [Review][Patch] `npx bmad-module-skill-forge` wrapper indirection mislabeled [docs/STABILITY.md § Programmatic API] — claim that `npx` invokes `tools/cli/skf-cli.js` directly; `package.json` `bin` actually points at `tools/skf-npx-wrapper.js` which execs `skf-cli.js`. One-line clarification: note the wrapper + exec indirection.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] `skf update` "no prior install" exit-0 undocumented [docs/STABILITY.md § User-Facing Command Output Formats] — `tools/cli/commands/update.js` exits 0 when no prior install is detected; contract only covers success + unhandled-failure. Add a "nothing to do (no prior install detected)" clause mirroring `skf uninstall`.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Inlined zod snippet drops `errorMap` option [docs/STABILITY.md § Skill Manifest & Frontmatter Contract] — transcription from `test/schema/agent.js:68-84` omits the `errorMap` on `z.enum`. Either include it verbatim or label the block "simplified — see `test/schema/agent.js` for exact source."
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Top-level flag vs per-subcommand-flag claim reads as contradictory [docs/STABILITY.md § CLI Command Surface] — "`-V, --version`" and "`-h, --help`" are committed at top level, then "Per-subcommand flags: none". Commander provides `-h/--help` on every subcommand by default. Clarify: note that `-h/--help` is also available per-subcommand (commander-default) and is covered, or reword to avoid the apparent contradiction.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] "Intermediate exit codes are not part of the contract" is confusing [docs/STABILITY.md § CLI Command Surface] — reads as if mid-execution exit codes somehow differ. Rewrite: "Only exit codes `0` (success) and `1` (unhandled failure) are reserved; future exit-code values may be added additively in minor releases."
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Forge-sidecar: "covered + @internal + stable across updates" is confusing [docs/STABILITY.md § Installation Layout and § @internal] — the directory is covered (existence + location), its internals are @internal, but "stable as a runtime contract between SKF versions during one install's lifetime (so `skf update` works)." Restate: `skf update` round-trips sidecar state between versions; the file formats inside are @internal (not directly consumable by downstream tools).
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Install layout "exactly the following paths" contradicts configurable path names [docs/STABILITY.md § Installation Layout] — the list commits `{skills_output_folder}/` (default `skills/`) and `{forge_data_folder}/` (default `forge-data/`) as user-configurable. Reword "exactly the following paths" → "the following paths (some names configurable at install time; defaults shown)".
- [ ] [Review][Patch] `platform-codes.yaml` "whole file is the contract" over-commits [docs/STABILITY.md § Installation Layout — per-IDE skill directories] — text asserts the YAML "is itself part of the v1.0.0 contract" then carves out two specific key paths. Other keys in the file are not part of the contract. Replace "the file is itself part of the v1.0.0 contract" with "the `platforms.<ide>.installer.target_dir` mapping in that file is part of the contract; other keys are `@internal`."
- [ ] [Review][Patch] `module` field ambiguity — consumer-supplied vs SKF-injected [docs/STABILITY.md § Skill Manifest & Frontmatter Contract] — schema lists `module` as a required field, prose says "the `module` field is always `skf` for SKF-authored manifests." Unclear whether consumers writing manifests must supply `module: skf` verbatim or whether SKF injects it. Clarify consumer-authoring expectation.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Missing issue-tracker URL [docs/STABILITY.md § How to Use This Contract] — "open an issue at the SKF issue tracker" is a dead-end pointer for a downstream consumer. Add the GitHub Issues URL.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] "User-cancel" for `skf install` not defined [docs/STABILITY.md § CLI Command Surface — Exit codes + § User-Facing Command Output Formats] — contract commits `install` to exit 0 on user-cancel without defining the cancel surface. Add a parenthetical naming the concrete cancel paths (Ctrl-C at a prompt, or declining a confirm step).
- [ ] [Review][Patch] `manifest.version` semantics unspecified [docs/STABILITY.md § Installation Layout] — manifest contract lists `version` as a covered top-level key but doesn't say what it means. Add: "`version` equals the SKF package version at install time — downstream tools may read it to detect the installed SKF version."

**Deferred (pre-existing or out-of-docs-scope — appended to `deferred-work.md`):**

- [x] [Review][Defer] `commands/install.js` silent exit 0 on `{success:false}` [tools/cli/commands/install.js:23] — deferred, code issue out of docs-only scope.
- [x] [Review][Defer] `package.json` `main` points at side-effectful CLI [package.json:28] — deferred, architectural choice predates this story.
- [x] [Review][Defer] Mixed RFC 2119 keyword conventions (SHALL / SHOULD / may / should) [docs/STABILITY.md — entire doc] — deferred, stylistic-prose hygiene.
- [x] [Review][Defer] `description` "trigger phrase" requirement under-specified [docs/STABILITY.md § Skill Manifest] — deferred, doc points at live examples in `src/skf-*/SKILL.md`; formal grammar belongs upstream at agentskills.io.
- [x] [Review][Defer] Caret-range guidance (`"^1.0.0"`) reads ahead of v1.0.0 actually shipping [docs/STABILITY.md § How to Use This Contract] — deferred, DRAFT banner covers pre-v1.0.0 context; Story 5.1 pre-flight audit owns the lock.

**Dismissed as noise (10):** DRAFT banner "undermines the contract" (AC #6 intentional); `require` side-effects framed as a programmatic surface (doc handles); unpinned agentskills.io spec (intentional "track upstream" design); `_bmad-output/` gitignored/not-shipped claim (trivially verifiable); `release:*` scripts excluded by fiat (accurate current framing); anchor `#internal--not-covered-by-semver` slug concerns (verified correct by Edge Case Hunter); README buries contract placement (AC #5 mandated ordering); "git log docs/STABILITY.md" historical-versions pointer (pedantic); no date on "locks at v1.0.0 cut" (DRAFT banner + Story 5.1 gate is the lock trigger, not a date); adding-optional-field-to-strict-zod "additive" framing (niche consumer-authoring angle).

Commander provides `-h/--help` on every subcommand by default. Clarify: note that `-h/--help` is also available per-subcommand (commander-default) and is covered, or reword to avoid the apparent contradiction.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] "Intermediate exit codes are not part of the contract" is confusing [docs/STABILITY.md § CLI Command Surface] — reads as if mid-execution exit codes somehow differ. Rewrite: "Only exit codes `0` (success) and `1` (unhandled failure) are reserved; future exit-code values may be added additively in minor releases."
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Forge-sidecar: "covered + @internal + stable across updates" is confusing [docs/STABILITY.md § Installation Layout and § @internal] — the directory is covered (existence + location), its internals are @internal, but "stable as a runtime contract between SKF versions during one install's lifetime (so `skf update` works)." Restate: `skf update` round-trips sidecar state between versions; the file formats inside are @internal (not directly consumable by downstream tools).
- [ ] [Review][Patch] Install layout "exactly the following paths" contradicts configurable path names [docs/STABILITY.md § Installation Layout] — the list commits `{skills_output_folder}/` (default `skills/`) and `{forge_data_folder}/` (default `forge-data/`) as user-configurable. Reword "exactly the following paths" → "the following paths (some names configurable at install time; defaults shown)".
- [ ] [Review][Patch] `platform-codes.yaml` "whole file is the contract" over-commits [docs/STABILITY.md § Installation Layout — per-IDE skill directories] — text asserts the YAML "is itself part of the v1.0.0 contract" then carves out two specific key paths. Other keys in the file are not part of the contract. Replace "the file is itself part of the v1.0.0 contract" with "the `platforms.<ide>.installer.target_dir` mapping in that file is part of the contract; other keys are `@internal`."
- [ ] [Review][Patch] `module` field ambiguity — consumer-supplied vs SKF-injected [docs/STABILITY.md § Skill Manifest & Frontmatter Contract] — schema lists `module` as a required field, prose says "the `module` field is always `skf` for SKF-authored manifests." - [ ] [Review][Patch] Missing issue-tracker URL [docs/STABILITY.md § How to Use This Contract] — "open an issue at the SKF issue tracker" is a dead-end pointer for a downstream consumer. Add the GitHub Issues URL.
- [ ] [Review][Patch] "User-cancel" for `skf install` not defined [docs/STABILITY.md § CLI Command Surface — Exit codes + § User-Facing Command Output Formats] — contract commits `install` to exit 0 on user-cancel without defining the cancel surface. - [ ] [Review][Patch] `manifest.version` semantics unspecified [docs/STABILITY.md § Installation Layout] — manifest contract lists `version` as a covered top-level key but doesn't say what it means. Add: "`version` equals the SKF package version at install time — downstream tools may read it to detect the installed SKF version."

## Deferred from: code review of 2-3-author-docs-stability-md-v1-0-0-public-api-contract (2026-04-21)

- **`commands/install.js:23` silent exit 0 on `{success: false}`** — `tools/cli/commands/install.js` only exits 1 when `result.success` is truthy; a falsy `success` falls through and the process exits 0 by default. This contradicts STABILITY.md's commitment "`skf install` SHALL exit `1` on unhandled failure." Code bug, not a docs issue. Fix belongs in a small follow-up or rolled into Story 5.1's pre-flight audit code-path review. Low probability in practice (the install code typically either throws or returns `{success: true}`), but a real contract gap.
- **`package.json` `main` points at side-effectful CLI entrypoint** — `package.json:28` has `"main": "tools/cli/skf-cli.js"`. The file calls `program.parse(process.argv)` at module top level; a `require('bmad-module-skill-forge')` runs the parser against the caller's `process.argv`. Standard npm convention is that `main` points at a library entry (exports), `bin` points at CLI entrypoints. Pre-existing architectural choice predating this story; STABILITY.md Path B (no programmatic API) handles the semantics, but the `main` field itself is a surface worth cleaning up pre-v1.0.0. Story 5.1 pre-flight audit or a standalone pre-v1.0.0 housekeeping story is the natural home.
- **Mixed RFC 2119 keyword conventions in STABILITY.md** — `docs/STABILITY.md` uses uppercase `SHALL` for some commitments and lowercase `should` / `may` elsewhere, without an RFC 2119 keyword preamble. Minor prose hygiene for a document that functions as a contract. Either add a "key words" preamble at the top or normalize to one style throughout. Defer to Story 5.1's pre-flight audit prose pass.
- **SKILL.md `description` "trigger phrase" requirement under-specified** — `docs/STABILITY.md § Skill Manifest & Frontmatter Contract` asserts the `description` field "terminates with an explicit trigger phrase" and points at `src/skf-*/SKILL.md` for live examples. A contract field defined by "go look at some files" is informal. The formal grammar belongs upstream at agentskills.io, not in SKF's contract — SKF's STABILITY.md already says "SKF tracks upstream rather than forking." Defer: if agentskills.io formalizes the grammar, link to the definition; otherwise leave the example-based reference.
- **Caret-range pinning guidance reads ahead of v1.0.0 actually shipping** — `docs/STABILITY.md § How to Use This Contract` tells consumers to pin `"^1.0.0"`, but SKF is at `0.10.0` when this DRAFT lands. The DRAFT banner covers the temporal gap, but a reader landing on the doc pre-v1.0.0 may pin against a version that doesn't exist yet. Defer to Story 5.1 pre-flight audit which owns the DRAFT-to-STABLE banner flip; the caret-range language is correct once v1.0.0 ships.
EOF
echo "appended"

Findings written to the story file and `deferred-work.md`.

> **Code review complete.** 5 `decision-needed`, 14 `patch`, 5 `defer`, 10 dismissed as noise.
>
> Findings written to the review findings section in `_bmad-output/implementation-artifacts/2-3-author-docs-stability-md-v1-0-0-public-api-contract.md`.

Now I need to resolve the 5 `decision-needed` findings. Each requires your judgment — the correct fix is ambiguous.

---

**D1. Npm tarball excludes referenced-as-contract files**

`docs/STABILITY.md` cites `test/schema/agent.js` and `../CHANGELOG.md` as part of the v1.0.0 contract, but `.npmignore:7,22` excludes both from the published tarball. A downstream consumer reading the doc from `node_modules/` gets broken references.

Options:
1. **Ship the referenced files** — whitelist via `package.json` `files[]` or add `!test/schema/**` + `!CHANGELOG.md` exceptions to `.npmignore`. Expands tarball but keeps contract self-contained.
2. **Absolute GitHub URLs** — replace relative refs with pinned `https://github.com/.../blob/v1.0.0/...` URLs. Contract survives in tarball but relies on GitHub availability.
3. **Declare GitHub-only** — add a line to STABILITY.md saying "this contract is published on GitHub; consumers reading from `node_modules/` should refer to github.com/..." — cheapest, honest.
4. **Defer to Story 5.1** — add a carry-forward flag; Story 5.1 pre-flight audit resolves before v1.0.0.
5. **Dismiss** — I'm misreading the concern; not worth acting on.

**HALT** — which option for D1?

content.text:
Here are my recommendations, with reasoning.

**D1 — I recommend Option 2 (absolute GitHub URLs), with a small tweak to D3 resolution.**

Key insight: the doc already *inlines* the zod schema, so `test/schema/agent.js` is a verification pointer, not required reading. And `CHANGELOG.md` is a pointer to release history — a URL works as well as a file. Option 2 keeps the contract self-contained for both GitHub and node_modules readers, doesn't bloat the tarball, and matches what most npm-published contract docs do. Option 1 ships files that otherwise have no business in the tarball; Option 3 is honest but kicks the problem to the reader.

**D2 — I recommend Option 2 (defer to Story 5.1).**

The Completion Notes already flagged this as a Story-5.1 handoff. Story 5.1's pre-flight audit re-reads STABILITY.md and flips DRAFT→STABLE — adding a sidebar entry in the same commit costs nothing and keeps this story's AC #8 envelope clean. One carry-forward line in deferred-work is the right shape.

**D3 — I recommend Option (a) (carve out `test/schema/**` as covered).**

One-line tweak to the @internal bullet: "Test fixtures and test file layout under `test/` — test organization is internal (**except `test/schema/**`, which defines schemas covered by this contract**)." Cheapest, preserves the existing framing, and reflects the actual intent (the schema *happens* to live under `test/` for historical reasons but is authoritative).

**D4 — I recommend Option (a) (add an Engine & Platform Compatibility subsection).**

This *is* a legit contract gap. `package.json` has a Node engine range (verify what it says). A short bucket #6 — "Engine & Platform Compatibility" — can commit: "supported Node.js major versions track `package.json` `engines.node`; dropping a major is breaking, adding one is additive. Linux/macOS/Windows all supported; dropping OS support is breaking." Docs-only, substantive, closes a gap a consumer would actually hit.

**D5 — I recommend Option (b) (soften to "best-effort state preservation").**

Current wording over-promises an invariant on an @internal surface with no test coverage. "`skf update` aims to preserve existing config and sidecar state across versions; breaking migrations, when needed, will be called out in `CHANGELOG.md`." Matches reality, doesn't require a test backfill, aligns with the "CHANGELOG flags breaking changes" convention.

---

**Summary of recommended route:** D1 → option 2, D2 → option 2 (defer), D3 → option (a), D4 → option (a), D5 → option (b).

All five resolutions are docs-only and stay within STABILITY.md (plus one deferred-work entry for D2). No code changes, no AC #8 violations.

**HALT** — accept these recommendations as a batch (reply "accept"), or call out specific ones to change?
